We now consider real-valued functions of several variables,
f : Rn →R,
x = (x1, . . . , xn).
In contrast to the one-dimensional case, there is no single notion of slope: the function may
increase in some directions and decrease in others.
Local variation is described through derivatives with respect to each coordinate direction.
Our goal is to extend the one-dimensional ideas by identifying a direction that summarizes
first-order behavior in all coordinates.
This requires assembling partial derivative information into a single object.

Gradient Descent
Let f : Rd →R be differentiable.
Starting from an initialization x0 ∈Rd, define the iterates
xk+1 = xk −ηk∇f(xk),
k = 0, 1, 2, . . .
where ηk > 0 is a step size.
For a fixed step size ηk = η,
xk+1 = xk −η∇f(xk).
A typical stopping condition is
∥∇f(xk)∥≤ε
or

24 / 36

The update
xk+1 = xk −η∇f(xk)
is simple, but its behavior can vary significantly in practice.
Convergence depends on:
the choice of step size η
the local geometry of the objective function
the scaling of different coordinates
Even for smooth functions, the sequence (xk) may:
converge slowly,
oscillate,
or stagnate far from an optimum.
These effects become more pronounced in high-dimensional settings.
25 / 36

Several structural issues limit the efficiency of basic gradient descent.
If the level sets of f are highly elongated, the gradient may point toward steep directions
rather than toward the minimum, leading to slow progress.
The same step size is applied to all coordinates, even if the function varies at very different
scales along different directions.
In nonconvex problems:
convergence to saddle points is possible,
different initializations may lead to different solutions.
These limitations motivate modified update rules and improved step-size strategies.
26 / 36

27 / 36
Momentum
Basic gradient descent updates the parameters using only the current gradient.
Momentum introduces a running velocity:
vk+1 = βvk + ∇f(xk),
xk+1 = xk −ηvk+1,
with β ∈(0, 1).
The update accumulates gradient information over time, leading to smoother trajectories.
This reduces oscillations and accelerates progress along directions of consistent descent.
28 / 36

Gradient descent applies the same step size to all coordinates:
xk+1 = xk −η∇f(xk).
When different coordinates have different scales, this can lead to inefficient zig-zagging
behavior.
A more general update takes the form
xk+1 = xk −η Pk∇f(xk),
where Pk is a positive definite matrix.
Such transformations rescale directions of descent to better match the geometry of the
objective.
29 / 36

In many problems, different coordinates evolve at different rates.
Adaptive methods adjust the step size during optimization, often on a per-coordinate basis.
Typical strategies:
decrease the step size over time,
normalize updates using past gradient magnitudes,
combine scaling with momentum.
These approaches reduce sensitivity to parameter tuning and improve robustness in
large-scale problems.

A fixed step size uses
ηk = η > 0.
This choice:
is simple and computationally cheap,
often works well in practice when properly tuned,
may lead to divergence or oscillations if η is too large.

Instead of using the full gradient, one can approximate it using a small subset of terms.
Let ik ∈{1, . . . , N} be a randomly selected index. An unbiased approximation of the
gradient is
gk = ∇fik(xk),
E[gk] = ∇f(xk).
This approximation is much cheaper to compute, but introduces randomness into the updates.
The optimization method trades exact descent for faster, noisier iterations.

unbiased,
noisy,
significantly cheaper to compute.

Compared to gradient descent:
each iteration is much cheaper,
updates are no longer deterministic,
the objective value may not decrease at every step.
The behavior of SGD depends critically on:

the structure of the objective function.

COMPUTER VISION — Final Project Brief Saint Joseph University · Spring 2026

Teams: up to 3 students
Duration: 1 month
Dataset: freely chosen (public benchmark or custom)
Language: Python or C++; any framework
Deliverables: GitHub repo, written report, demo video, live defense

Design and implement a Computer Vision system that solves a real or well-defined
problem of your choice. You are free to use classical approaches, deep learning, or a
combination of both. You are not required to cover all CV topics — depth and quality on a
focused problem is more valuable than a shallow treatment of many techniques.

Choose any problem that can be addressed with computer vision. Examples include (but
are not limited to):
Object detection or recognition in a specific domain (medical, industrial, aerial,
etc.)
Scene segmentation for autonomous driving or robotics
Multi-object tracking in video
Image classification with limited data (few-shot, self-supervised)
Anomaly or defect detection
Pose estimation or action recognition
Any other problem you find meaningful and technically challenging
Your chosen problem must be specific enough that results can be measured quantitatively.

Your project must include at least the following:
A clear problem formulation and motivation
A chosen approach — classical CV, deep learning (CNN, transformer, etc.), or hybrid
— with justification
A training or evaluation pipeline on a real dataset
Quantitative evaluation with appropriate metrics for your task (accuracy, mAP,
mIoU, MOTA, etc.)
At least one experiment showing the effect of a design choice (architecture, loss,
augmentation, threshold, etc.)
Visualization of results (predictions, failure cases, attention maps, etc.)

Any public dataset or data you collect yourself
Must be clearly documented: source, size, class distribution, split strategy
(train/val/test)
Minimum 500 labelled samples; more is expected for detection and segmentation
tasks

Photosynthesis is the process plants use to convert light energy into chemical energy.
This process allows plants to produce glucose, which stores energy that supports growth and survival.
Photosynthesis mainly takes place in the chloroplasts of plant cells.
Chlorophyll, the green pigment inside chloroplasts, helps capture sunlight from the environment.
Water absorbed by the roots and carbon dioxide from the air are key inputs used during photosynthesis.
Using these inputs and sunlight, plants produce glucose and release oxygen as an output.
Photosynthesis is important because it supports plant life, contributes oxygen to the atmosphere, and helps sustain food chains.

For each key-point (corner) we need to find its best scale which have maximum value (cornerness measure). As cyber threats grow more sophisticated, traditional rule-based security systems have proven insufficient, driving researchers to explore artificial intelligence (AI) as a stronger alternative. This literature review examines three sources: “Threat Detection Driven by Artificial Intelligence” by Chen et al. (2024), “Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity” by Wirkuttis and Klein (2017), and “XAI for Cybersecurity” by Srivastava et al. (2022). Although each source approaches the topic differently — covering ML algorithms, security frameworks, and model explainability respectively — they collectively suggest that AI significantly enhances cybersecurity capabilities, but that its effective deployment depends on addressing persistent challenges around data quality, transparency, and human oversight.

All three articles agree that conventional cybersecurity tools are no longer adequate. Chen et al. (2024) explain that attacks have evolved from simple viruses into advanced persistent threats and ransomware, rendering signature-based detection — which depends on recognizing known threats — ineffective against new variants. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) add that traditional intrusion detection systems suffer from low detection rates, slow throughput, and an inability to adapt to changing environments without constant human adjustment. Srivastava et al. (2022) frame the consequences in concrete terms, noting that the average cost of a data breach has reached millions of dollars globally, and that even established frameworks like those from NIST and ISO are increasingly bypassed by attackers. The agreement across all three sources — spanning seven years of research — signals that the field
chunk.section_heading: The Failure of Traditional Security Tools

As digital infrastructure becomes increasingly central to daily life, the security systems designed to protect it face mounting pressure from threats that are more frequent, sophisticated, and difficult to predict. Researchers have turned to artificial intelligence as a promising avenue for strengthening cybersecurity practices. This literature review draws on three key sources: "Threat Detection Driven by Artificial Intelligence" by Chen et al. (2024), "Artificial Intelligence in Cybersecurity" by Wirkuttis and Klein (2017), and "XAI for Cybersecurity" by Srivastava et al. (2022). While these studies differ in focus, they share a common thread: AI holds transformative potential for cybersecurity, yet demands careful consideration of its limitations, particularly around transparency and human oversight.

Body Paragraph 1: Why Traditional Security Tools Are No Longer Sufficient
A central concern shared across the three articles is that conventional cybersecurity tools have become incapable of keeping pace with today's threat environment. Chen et al. (2024) describe how attacks have shifted from simple malware to threats like ransomware and advanced persistent threats, rendering signature-based detection systems — built around the assumption of known threats — largely ineffective. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) reinforce this, noting that static systems cannot self-adjust to unfamiliar attack patterns, and that human response alone introduces dangerous delays. Srivastava et al. (2022) contextualize the problem further, framing the urgency to adopt AI as a direct consequence of how badly conventional tools have struggled. The consistency of this critique across all three sources signals that

dopt AI as a direct consequence of how badly conventional tools have struggled. The consistency of this critique across all three sources signals that the field has largely moved past debating whether traditional methods are adequate, and toward determining what should replace them.

Having established the gap left by traditional tools, the three articles collectively make a strong case for AI as its most viable replacement. Chen et al. (2024) highlight how machine learning enables real-time detection of previously unseen threats through behavioral analysis and network traffic monitoring, directly improving incident response quality. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) take a broader view, emphasizing AI's ability to process vast volumes of security data and surface patterns no human team could realistically manage, pushing organizations toward a more anticipatory security model. Srivastava et al. (2022) add that AI can also track individual user behavior to flag deviations indicative of a breach — a dimension traditional systems entirely lack. Though each source highlights a different application, all three point to the same shift: security is moving from passive,

onal systems entirely lack. Though each source highlights a different application, all three point to the same shift: security is moving from passive, rules-based gatekeeping toward active, intelligent threat identification.
Body Paragraph 3: AI's Limitations and the Case for Explainability
None of the three sources treat AI as a problem-free solution. Chen et al. (2024) caution that AI models are only as reliable as their training data, and poor data management can significantly degrade performance. Wirkuttis and Klein (2017) argue that AI still lacks the contextual judgment needed to operate independently, making human involvement essential. Srivastava et al. (2022) offer the most detailed critique, identifying inaccurate outputs, high computational costs, and vulnerability to adversarial manipulation as key concerns. Most critically, they highlight that most AI systems function as

Most critically, they highlight that most AI systems function as opaque black boxes, offering no explanation for their decisions, and propose Explainable AI (XAI) as the solution. Though the other sources do not use the term, their shared emphasis on human collaboration points toward the same need. Together, these perspectives make clear that the next frontier in AI-driven cybersecurity is not just higher accuracy, but greater accountability.
chunk.section_heading: Body Paragraph 2: How AI Strengthens Detection and Response

chunk.chunk_text: Taken together, the three articles present a clear picture of where cybersecurity stands and where it is headed. Conventional tools have proven inadequate against modern attacks, and AI has emerged as a necessary alternative offering faster detection and greater adaptability. However, the literature also highlights real challenges — poor data, limited autonomy, and a lack of transparency all pose risks. The common thread across all three sources is the need for AI systems that are not only powerful but also explainable and aligned with human judgment. Future research could investigate how explainability tools can be embedded into active cybersecurity systems without introducing performance trade-offs or new vulnerabilities.

Smart Teacher Assistant is an AI-powered backend for education workflows.

The application provides agent endpoints for summarizing documents, creating slide decks, and generating quizzes.

The chat endpoint answers questions using retrieved document context. It should only answer from indexed documents and say "Not found in document" when the answer is missing from the context.

The retrieval flow indexes local files from the knowledge base folder, splits them into chunks, stores embeddings in a local JSON vector store, and returns the most relevant chunks for a user question.
